mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Hong Kong on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, on the south coast of China. Hong Kong is a Special Administrative Region (SAR) of China: officially part of China but with its own laws, money, government and passport 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is Hong Kong, really?
mapQuestions.answer: A peninsula and 263 islands at the mouth of the Pearl River. About 7.5 million people packed into one of the most densely populated places on Earth.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Hong Kong speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Cantonese (a Chinese language different from Mandarin), Mandarin Chinese, and English are all official. Most younger Hong Kongers speak all three.

mapQuestions.question: Why is Hong Kong different from mainland China?
mapQuestions.answer: Hong Kong was a British colony from 1841 to 1997. When it returned to China, the agreement was "One Country, Two Systems": Hong Kong keeps its own laws, money, courts and freedoms for 50 years.

mapQuestions.question: How densely packed is Hong Kong?
mapQuestions.answer: About 7,000 people per square kilometre across the whole region (compared to ~430 for England). But because 75% of Hong Kong is mountains and country parks, the urban districts feel even more crowded.

foods.name: Dim sum
foods.description: A whole style of eating: dozens of small bamboo-steamer dishes (siu mai dumplings, har gow prawn dumplings, char siu bao pork buns, chicken feet, custard tarts), brought around tables in carts and ticked off on a paper. Eaten for brunch with hot tea.

foods.name: Roast goose
foods.description: Cantonese-style roast goose: crispy mahogany-coloured skin, juicy meat, served over rice with a sweet plum sauce. Restaurants like Yat Lok have a Michelin star for it.

foods.name: Wonton noodles
foods.description: Thin yellow egg noodles in a clear pork-and-shrimp broth, with little wontons (shrimp-filled dumplings) and a sprinkle of yellow chives. The classic Hong Kong noodle soup.

foods.name: Pineapple bun (boluo bao)
foods.description: A sweet bun with a sugar-crust top that looks like a pineapple (no actual pineapple inside). Best eaten warm with a thick slice of butter cut into the middle.

foods.name: Egg tart (daan tat)
foods.description: A small Portuguese-influenced tart with flaky pastry and a wobbly silky custard centre. A Hong Kong afternoon snack standard.

foods.name: Curry fish balls
foods.description: Bouncy fishballs on skewers, simmered in a mild curry-and-coconut sauce. Sold at every street-food cart. The Hong Kong after-school snack.

scavengerItems.label: The Hong Kong skyline at night with the Victoria Harbour light show "Symphony of Lights"
A double-decker tram on Hong Kong Island ("Ding Ding")
The green-and-white Star Ferry crossing Victoria Harbour
The Big Buddha (Tian Tan Buddha) on Lantau Island, sitting 34 m tall on a lotus throne
A wet market with live seafood (the city is a serious seafood town)
A red Hong Kong taxi (Hong Kong Island/Kowloon) or a blue one (Lantau) or a green one (New Territories)
A bamboo steamer full of dim sum dumplings
Neon signs in Chinese characters (an iconic Hong Kong sight, slowly disappearing)
The MTR subway (one of the most efficient in the world)
Dragon dance at Chinese New Year
The Wishing Tree at Lam Tsuen, hung with red prayer ribbons
The hike up the Peak with the city skyline below

geoMatches.description: The original British colony part. Central business district, the Peak, Victoria Harbour skyline, the famous Star Ferry pier.
The peninsula on the mainland side of the harbour. Older, denser, more local feel. The waterfront promenade has the best view of Hong Kong Island's skyline.
The rural and suburban areas inland from Kowloon. Walled Hakka villages, country parks, hiking trails, and 70% of Hong Kong's land area.
The biggest island. Home to the Big Buddha, the Po Lin Monastery, Tai O fishing village built on stilts, Hong Kong Disneyland, and the international airport.
A small fishing-village island, with no cars, ferries to Hong Kong Island, and the famous annual "Bun Festival" with towers of buns and pole-climbing competitions.
The 552 m peak above Hong Kong Island, with the most famous skyline view in the city. Reached by the steep Peak Tram funicular.

senses.smell: Soy sauce, hot wok oil and ginger from every street kitchen, incense smoke at temples, sea salt at Victoria Harbour, sweet egg-tart pastry baking, the metal-and-electricity smell of the MTR…
senses.hear: Fast Cantonese flying everywhere, "M goi!" and "Nei hou!", the trundle of double-decker trams, MTR chimes, the clink of bamboo steamer lids being lifted at dim sum, the ring of mahjong tiles at a teahouse…
senses.taste: Hot prawn dumplings, crispy roast goose, sweet milk tea (Hong Kong-style with evaporated milk), bouncy fishballs in curry sauce, the silky custard of an egg tart…
senses.feel: Soupy summer humidity, the cool of an MTR carriage, the bounce of a tram, the steam of a dim sum bamboo basket, the smooth wood of chopsticks in your hand…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why was Hong Kong British?
stories.body: After the First Opium War (a brutal trade conflict between Britain and China in the 1840s), the British forced China to hand over Hong Kong Island as a colony in 1842. They added Kowloon in 1860 and leased the much bigger New Territories for 99 years in 1898. For 156 years Hong Kong grew under British rule as a free-trade port, banking centre and refuge for Chinese fleeing political turmoil on the mainland. The lease on the New Territories ran out in 1997. The British government handed all of Hong Kong back to China that summer, with an agreement that Hong Kong would keep its own legal system, money, freedoms and lifestyle ("One Country, Two Systems") until 2047. Hong Kong's relationship with the rest of China continues to evolve.

stories.question: What is dim sum?
stories.body: "Dim sum" (literally "touch the heart") is a southern Chinese (especially Cantonese) way of eating: dozens of small steamer dishes, served all together, eaten with hot tea, usually for late breakfast or lunch. It started in southern Chinese teahouses about 1,000 years ago as snacks served to travellers on the Silk Road. Classic dim sum dishes include har gow (steamed prawn dumplings), siu mai (open-topped pork-and-prawn dumplings), char siu bao (steamed barbecued-pork buns), egg tarts, lotus-leaf-wrapped sticky rice. Traditional teahouses bring the dishes around on trolleys, and you point at what you want. The total is added up at the end by counting the empty bamboo baskets. Eating dim sum is one of the most loved Hong Kong rituals.

stories.question: How tall is "the Peak"?
stories.body: Victoria Peak (552 m) is the highest hill on Hong Kong Island. Since the early colonial days, it has been the most prestigious place to live: cooler air, better breezes, escape from the city. The Peak Tram, a steep funicular railway, opened in 1888 to bring residents up and down (and is still running, the world's oldest funicular). At the top, the Sky Terrace gives one of the most famous views in the world: the entire Hong Kong skyline below, Victoria Harbour, Kowloon, and the New Territories mountains in the distance. Best visited at sunset, when the skyscrapers light up and the harbour fills with boats.

stories.question: How big is the Big Buddha?
stories.body: On a small hill on Lantau Island sits the "Tian Tan Buddha", a giant bronze statue of the seated Buddha. Built in 1993, the statue is 34 metres tall (including the lotus throne) and weighs 250 tonnes. You climb 268 steps to reach the base. The Buddha faces north, toward the rest of China, and is surrounded by six smaller bronze figures presenting offerings. Below the Buddha sits the Po Lin Monastery, one of Hong Kong's most important Buddhist temples. The whole site is reachable by the Ngong Ping 360 cable car from Tung Chung, with spectacular views of Lantau, the sea, and the airport below.

stories.question: Why do Hong Kong streets have so many neon signs?
stories.body: For decades, Hong Kong was the global capital of neon street signs: hand-bent glass tubes filled with glowing gas, hanging out into the street from every restaurant, mahjong parlour, pawnshop and noodle stall. By the 1990s there were over 100,000 neon signs in Hong Kong. The street scene became one of the most photographed cityscapes in the world, used in films like "Blade Runner" and "Chungking Express". Stricter building safety laws in the 2000s have led to many signs being taken down. There are now only a few thousand left, and Hong Kong artists and historians are trying to preserve some of the best as cultural heritage.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Hong Kong so far?
Have you said "m goi" or "do je" yet?
Skyline, harbour, market, temple, which has felt the most "Hong Kong"?
How does Hong Kong's pace compare to anywhere else we have been?
Have you ridden the Star Ferry across the harbour? What did you see?
Hong Kong is technically part of China but feels different. How does that feel as you walk around?

wordSearch.hint: Hong Kong foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Black kite
animals.description: Big brown birds of prey soaring above Victoria Harbour and the city, often spotted from rooftops and ferries.

animals.name: Common Mormon butterfly
animals.description: Big black-and-white butterflies common in the Hong Kong Park and country parks.

animals.name: Tokay gecko
animals.description: Big tropical geckos in older buildings and country-park trees. Famous loud "to-kay! to-kay!" call.

animals.name: Chinese white dolphin (pink dolphin)
animals.description: A pale-pink dolphin native to the Pearl River estuary near Lantau. Endangered, with maybe 50 left in Hong Kong waters.

animals.name: Wild ox
animals.description: Feral cattle wander Lantau's south coast (Pui O), descendants of farm cows abandoned decades ago.

animals.name: Crested goshawk
animals.description: A bird of prey that has adapted to Hong Kong's urban parks, sometimes nesting on apartment blocks.

animals.name: Saltwater crocodile
animals.description: In 2003 a saltwater crocodile turned up in a Hong Kong river and stayed for 5 years before being caught. "Pui Pui" is now an unofficial city legend.
